title: SuperBEST Cost Analysis — Core Finance
note: Parameters and constants are free (0 cost).

equations.name: Compound interest (discrete)
equations.key_insight: Discrete compounding requires pow on a sum, contributing 3+3=6 nodes for the exponential part.

equations.name: Continuous compounding
equations.key_insight: Continuous compounding (5 nodes) is mathematically cheaper than discrete (8 nodes). exp costs 1 vs pow costs 3; the (1+r) add is also avoided.

equations.name: Present value
equations.key_insight: div costs only 1 node vs mul costs 2, so PV (7n) is cheaper than compound interest A (8n) despite the same base structure.

equations.name: Net present value (N cash flows)

breakdown.note: With (1+r) computed once and shared; each period t needs pow(1+r, t) independently

equations.key_insight: NPV scales as O(N) with N cash flows — same asymptotic scaling as N-compartment pharmacokinetics models. Each discount factor (1+r)^t is an independent pow call (different exponent t), so sharing saves only the add(1,r) = 3 nodes across all terms.

equations.name: Gordon growth model (dividend discount)
equations.key_insight: Minimal cost formula — closed-form perpetuity requires only subtraction and division.

equations.name: CAPM (Capital Asset Pricing Model)
equations.key_insight: Linear pricing model; add costs 3 (positive add) while sub costs 2, making the final summation the most expensive single step.

equations.name: Sharpe ratio
equations.key_insight: Same minimal cost as Gordon growth model (3 nodes). Risk-adjusted return collapses to sub + div.

equations.name: Macaulay duration (bond)
equations.formula: D = sum_t [ t * CF_t * (1+r)^(-t) ] / sum_t [ CF_t * (1+r)^(-t) ]

breakdown.note: Denominator reuses CF_t * discount factors; numerator adds one mul(t, ...) per term

equations.total_nodes_formula: O(N) — approximately 10N nodes for N cash flows (8N denom + 2N extra for t weighting + 1 for final div)
equations.key_insight: Duration is the most expensive per-term formula due to negative exponent (pow with neg_t) and double mul weighting. Denominator alone matches discrete compound interest (8n per term).

equations.name: Log return
equations.key_insight: Optimal form ln(Pt/Pt-1) uses div(1n) + ln(1n) = 2 nodes total. The naive form ln(Pt) - ln(Pt-1) costs 4 nodes. SuperBEST prefers the ratio-then-log form — a 2x cost reduction via algebraic identity ln(a/b) = ln(a) - ln(b).

equations.name: Continuously compounded yield

breakdown.note: 1/T treated as a free constant parameter when T is fixed

equations.key_insight: When T is a fixed parameter (free), cost is 2 nodes — identical to log return. When T is variable, mul(1/T, ln) adds 2 nodes for 4 total. The div+ln pattern recurs across financial log formulas.

cross_domain_findings.finding: Continuous vs discrete compounding cost asymmetry
cross_domain_findings.detail: Continuous compounding (5 nodes) is cheaper than discrete compounding (8 nodes) in SuperBEST. The savings come from: (1) exp costs 1 vs pow costs 3, and (2) the add(1,r) step (3 nodes) is eliminated. This formalizes why exponential growth is the 'natural' representation for compounding.

cross_domain_findings.finding: O(N) scaling: NPV mirrors N-compartment pharmacokinetics
cross_domain_findings.detail: NPV with N cash flows scales as O(7N) nodes, identical in asymptotic structure to N-compartment pharmacokinetic models. Both require one independent discount/decay factor per period/compartment with no cross-term sharing possible.

cross_domain_findings.finding: Minimal finance formulas cluster at 2-3 nodes
cross_domain_findings.detail: Gordon growth model (3n), Sharpe ratio (3n), log return (2n), and continuously compounded yield (2n with fixed T) represent the irreducible core of finance. All are single-operation compositions of sub/div/ln.

The algebraic identity ln(a/b) = ln(a) - ln(b) applied in reverse (grouping before logging) reduces log return from 4 nodes to 2 nodes. SuperBEST naturally discovers this form as the minimum-cost representation.

cross_domain_findings.finding: add costs more than div in SuperBEST
cross_domain_findings.detail: add = 3 nodes, div = 1 node. This is counterintuitive relative to computational complexity but reflects operator expressiveness. Formulas that replace add with div (e.g., log return using ratio form) are cheaper.
